body:
The `updateLibrary` is a high-level API, same as `updateScene`, which also takes `appState` (which can alter UI state) and `commitToHistory`. It's about usefulness and semantics. Since opening the library menu is currently tied to updating the library, it makes sense to expose it.

The alternative is to force host apps to do an extra:

```
excalidrawAPI.updateScene({ appState: { isLibraryOpen: true } });
```

I don't care much, but I think the argument here and in your other comments stems from wanting to make this API low-level, which we don't need to.
author: dwelle

body:
We have a problem with grapheme clusters or multi-codepoint-letters or whatever you want to call them. The core issue with the current code is that  `[...'🤌🏼👌🏿']` evaluates to `[ '🤌', '🏼', '👌', '🏿' ]` 

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/9257001/167246490-05ecd607-74e9-4d6d-ab9a-741cbb0d01a6.mov
author: tom-sherman

body:
I'm assuming that the text selection API also works on graphemes ie. selecting `🤌🏼` gives you a range of length 1 not 2. If this is the case I think the only solution is to have both `textElement.colorRanges` and `getLineGroupedRanges` work off of graphemes too.

In any case somewhere is going to have to be responsible for splitting by grapheme which probably means we need to pull in https://github.com/orling/grapheme-splitter

Edit: Text selection doesn't work on graphemes. Selecting any standard emoji gives you a range of length 2.
author: tom-sherman

body:
Ah, we should use https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/Intl/Segmenter

Would need to include a polyfill for firefox tho, not sure on the strategy of supporty/polyfills in this repo. Can anyone comment?
author: tom-sherman

body:
haven't reviewed yet so unclear what issues you're facing exactly, but do we even need to be aware of graphemes? Can't we just save the exact offsets of a given selection range? I assume the answer is we can't mainly due to how it would work alongside line breaks when bound text comes into play?

If not, then the key is not to use array spread `[...string]` because it splits on code points (thus is potentially multi-byte), and instead use `string.split('')` which splits on bytes — then you can just index into the string as normal.
author: dwelle

body: The problem with doing `.split('')` here is that once they're split it's not clear how to recombine the codepoints to form the original graphemes. This is also why we need to be aware of graphemes in the first place, not sure you can apply a colour range without worrying about them because a range applies to graphemes - not codepoints.
author: tom-sherman

body:
Depending on what you do afterwards, you should still be able to treat the string as a black box and it won't really matter?

For example, if you have `Peru 🇵🇪`, and the user selected the flag emoji, the range would be `[5, 9]` (the flag is 4 bytes). You split which gets you:

```
const bytes = ['P', 'e', 'r', 'u', ' ', '\uD83C', '\uDDF5', '\uD83C', '\uDDEA']
```

You extract the flag by slicing it out:

```
const selection = bytes.slice(5, 9);
```

You can then `selection.join('')` which gets you the 🇵🇪 grapheme back.
author: dwelle
